metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit-test target in the Tests AC that verifies `rebar` ticket ops (close/annotate) are called on successful atomic submit, e.g. assert the rebar mock receives `close(ticket_id)` after a successful tip submit.
findings.finding: The ticket close/annotate step ('close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops') in AC3 has no associated test or proving command anywhere in the Tests AC, leaving this behavior unverified by the plan.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit (third bullet) — 'On success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and correct the atomic-submit mechanism in AC3: specify that atomicity comes from `submitWholeTopic = true` + shared topic (not bare relation-chain ancestry), assert that `rebase:chain` outputs or the autolander assigns a shared topic to all chain members, and require the implementation to verify topic membership (or the absence of multiple topics) before calling `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the chain tip 'submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically' via 'relation-chain semantics'. This is mechanistically incorrect as stated: Gerrit's atomic multi-change submit relies on `submitWholeTopic = true` (confirmed active in the live config and ADR-0040), which operates on shared *topic*, not relation-chain ancestry. Relation-chain ancestry and topic membership are orthogonal in Gerrit. If chain members do not share a topic, submitting the tip leaves ancestors in the 'open' state — a partial stack land that violates R3 and potentially R5. The plan never asserts that `rebase:chain` assigns a shared topic or that the autolander verifies topic membership before submitting.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit (AC3)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical in AC3 with: 'under FFO + `submitWholeTopic = true` (already enabled server-wide), submitting the tip atomically lands all chain members only if they share a common topic — the autolander MUST verify all members carry the same topic before submit, and must assign/assert the topic at queue-entry time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live end-to-end check (or explicitly defer to a named child ticket with rationale) that exercises a ≥2-member chain where one member initially lacks a fresh Verified, then gains it, and the stack submits atomically — proving the multi-member fresh-Verified path runs in production.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end check covers only a 'single-change stack', but the plan's core invariant (AC1) is the multi-member chain case where ALL members must carry a fresh Verified on their post-rebase SHAs. Under E6(f), the new multi-member fresh-Verified code path is never exercised end-to-end in production conditions — only via unit tests that can be satisfied with mocks/stubs. Green unit tests plus a signed completion verdict are not sufficient to prove the chain-wide invariant holds on the live Gerrit instance.
findings.location: AC: Tests (last bullet) — 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Tests AC: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end AND one real two-member chain stack where member[0] initially has no fresh Verified, CI completes, then the stack submits atomically via a single tip submit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test criterion covering the `rebar` ticket close/annotate call: at minimum a unit test that verifies tickets are closed on successful submit and that a rebar failure is handled gracefully (does not silently swallow errors or leave tickets in a bad state).
findings.finding: The 'Ancestor-atomic submit' AC specifies closing/annotating associated tickets via `import rebar` ticket ops on success, but the Tests AC has no corresponding test for this behavior — it only covers the all-members-fresh rule, the bounded-await timeout→hand-back, and a live single-change-stack land. Ticket-op correctness (including the case where submit succeeds but rebar call fails) is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: AC: 'Ancestor-atomic submit' vs AC: 'Tests'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests AC, append: 'unit-test that on a successful submit the associated ticket(s) are closed/annotated via rebar, and that a rebar failure after a successful submit is surfaced (not silently ignored).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the live-check AC to include at least one real two-member chain stack that exercises fresh-Verified await on both members and confirms atomic all-or-nothing submit.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end check is scoped to 'one real single-change stack', leaving the novel and risky multi-member chain scenario (all members must carry fresh Verified on distinct post-rebase SHAs before a single tip submit) without empirical validation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Tests' bullet: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (unit or live) covering the `import rebar` ticket-annotation path: at minimum a happy-path assertion that the ticket op is invoked after a successful submit, and at least one failure-mode (e.g., rebar raises, no ticket associated).
findings.finding: The Tests AC specifies no test whatsoever for the `import rebar` ticket close/annotate behavior that is triggered on successful submit. This new user-facing side-effect (ticket is closed/annotated) has neither a happy-path unit test nor a failure-path test (e.g., rebar call fails, ticket already closed, no associated ticket).
findings.location: AC: 'Ancestor-atomic submit' bullet and 'Tests' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests AC, add: 'unit-test that a successful submit triggers the rebar ticket-close/annotate call (assert call is made with correct args); and that a rebar failure is logged and does not crash the loop'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live end-to-end acceptance criterion for a multi-member chain (N≥2): the check must confirm that (a) submit is blocked while any member lacks a fresh Verified on its post-rebase SHA and (b) submit proceeds once all members carry that label against real Gerrit.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end check covers only a single-change stack. The defining new behavior of this ticket — iterating over ALL chain members to verify each holds a fresh Verified +1 on its own post-rebase SHA — is exercised live only for N=1, where the per-member iteration loop trivially runs once. The multi-member fresh-Verified path (N≥2) is tested exclusively through unit/mock coverage, which presumably stubs the Gerrit label-query call and never hits the real API.
findings.location: AC: 'Tests' bullet — 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Tests AC: 'a live check also verifies a two-member chain: (i) submit is held while member-2 lacks a fresh Verified after rebase, (ii) submit proceeds once both members have fresh Verified, (iii) both changes appear merged atomically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify — with a codebase citation, Gerrit docs reference, or new e2e test — whether a bare `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` submits relation-chain ancestors atomically independent of `submitWholeTopic`; if `submitWholeTopic` is the actual R3 mechanism, confirm that all chain members share the required topic and document which mechanism the implementation relies on.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'under FFO with relation-chain semantics this submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically (all-or-nothing)' — but ADR-0040 attributes the multi-change atomic landing guarantee (R3) to `change.submitWholeTopic = true`, not to relation-chain submit semantics. These are distinct Gerrit mechanisms: a bare `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` may or may not propagate to relation-chain ancestors in an all-or-nothing manner; the codebase contains no e2e test or ADR passage that confirms this for a multi-member relation chain. The plan treats this as a verified fact without grounding.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit bullet: 'a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the chain tip; under FFO with relation-chain semantics this submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically (all-or-nothing)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note (or ADR amendment) clarifying that the atomic ancestor submit relies on `change.submitWholeTopic = true` (already set server-wide) with all chain members sharing a common topic — OR cite the Gerrit REST API docs showing that `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` propagates to relation-chain ancestors without topic involvement. If both mechanisms apply, name both explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **[T3]** Verify against Gerrit REST API docs (or a live probe) that `POST /a/changes/{id}/submit` on a relation-chain tip atomically submits all ancestors in a single all-or-nothing operation under FFO, and distinguish this from topic-based atomicity (`submitWholeTopic`); add a code comment or ADR note citing the specific Gerrit behavior.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` causes ancestor-atomic ('all-or-nothing') submit via 'relation-chain semantics' under FFO. This is a NEW endpoint surface not implemented anywhere in the codebase (gerrit_client.py has no submit method). The all-or-nothing ancestor-atomic claim for a single tip submit in a relation chain — as distinct from topic-based atomicity (`submitWholeTopic = true`) — is an unverified Gerrit capability claim. In practice, Gerrit submits relation-chain ancestors when submitting a tip, but the all-or-nothing guarantee (vs. partial submit if some ancestor is independently submittable) depends on server behavior that is not confirmed in any repo artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment in the implementation citing the Gerrit REST API docs section on submit (rest-api-changes.html#submit-change), and confirm via a live integration test or Gerrit source that relation-chain ancestors are included atomically. If they are not, wrap ancestor submits in a topic and rely on `submitWholeTopic = true` instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **[T3]** Name the specific `import rebar` write-path function(s) used for ticket close/annotate on successful submit (e.g., `rebar.transition()`), and state whether the autolander process has the rebar ticket worktree initialized or uses a subprocess/CLI call instead.
findings.finding: The plan names `import rebar` ticket ops for closing/annotating tickets on success but does not specify which specific rebar API surface is used (e.g., `rebar.transition()`, `rebar.close_ticket()`, or a subprocess call to the CLI). The `import rebar` library has a write path (`_lib_writes.py`, `transition_close.py`) but the specific function signatures, required config (repo_root, ticket ID extraction from change metadata), and whether the autolander's environment has the rebar ticket store accessible are unconfirmed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit AC bullet ('close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact `import rebar` operation(s) called on submit success: name the function/CLI command, specify how a Gerrit change-id maps to a rebar ticket_id, and clarify whether 'annotate' means a Gerrit comment, a rebar comment, a rebar label, or a transition — co-located in AC-3.
findings.finding: AC-3 mandates 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops' on successful submit, but the plan never defines which rebar operation to call, what parameters it takes, or how to map a Gerrit change-id to a rebar ticket_id. This is a referenced sub-agent/API with an undefined protocol.
findings.location: AC — Ancestor-atomic submit

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact Gerrit API query and field comparison used to determine 'fresh Verified': which endpoint (e.g. `GET /changes/{id}?o=DETAILED_LABELS`), which fields (`current_revision`, `labels.Verified.all[].permitted_voting_range` / patchset number), and the rule for distinguishing a fresh cast from a carried vote, co-located with the AC-1 schema.
findings.finding: The 'fresh Verified per member' await loop references detecting a `Verified +1` on the 'exact rebased patchset SHA', but the plan never specifies the decision rule — which Gerrit API fields to compare, how to enumerate per-member votes, and how to distinguish a freshly-cast vote from a carried/copied one. The schema the await loop operates on (current_revision vs. vote's patchset, the GET /changes query shape) is absent.
findings.location: AC — Fresh Verified (per member)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'matching `land`'s timeout' with a concrete shared constant (e.g., define `AUTOLANDER_VERIFIED_TIMEOUT_S = 1800` in a config module that both S2c's loop and the future S4 `land` command import) so the constraint is enforceable rather than documentary.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts the await timeout defaults to '30 min, configurable, matching `land`'s timeout' — but the `land` command does not exist in the codebase (it is the yet-to-be-built S4 agent command). No `land` command, no `LAND_TIMEOUT` constant, and no 30-min timeout value was found anywhere in src/rebar/_commands/ or related files. The 'matching' claim cannot be verified because the referent does not yet exist, making this a dangling forward-reference that could result in a mismatched timeout if S4 chooses a different value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Bounded await: 'default 30 min, configurable, matching `land`'s timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a shared `VERIFIED_AWAIT_TIMEOUT_S: int = 1800` in `infra/autolander/config.py` (or equivalent) and reference it explicitly in this AC rather than 'matching land's timeout'. When S4 is built, import the same constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which specific functions/classes in `infra/autolander/loop.py` S2c owns (e.g., `await_fresh_verified()`, `submit_tip()`) and acknowledge that S2a and S2b own other sections of the same file, so each sibling can be implemented and reviewed independently.
findings.finding: S2a (89f9-b381-60e2-4802), S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee), and S2c (this ticket) all name `infra/autolander/loop.py` as their edit target. The plan does not acknowledge this shared-file situation or specify which sections/functions each sibling owns, creating an unaddressed coordination risk where concurrent or sequential implementations collide on the same module.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/autolander/loop.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Symbols added by this ticket' subsection under Files listing the new function names S2c contributes (e.g., `await_fresh_verified(wipChain) -> None`, `submit_tip(tip_id) -> None`), and note that S2b's hand-back entry point is called by reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for `import rebar` ticket close/annotate failure after a successful Gerrit submit: at minimum, state whether it is best-effort (log + continue), whether a Gerrit comment is posted as a backstop, and what happens when no ticket_id is associated with a change.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback for the `import rebar` ticket close/annotate step failing after a successful (irreversible) Gerrit submit. The submit success path has a required post-step (ticket ops) with no error-handling protocol.
findings.location: AC — Ancestor-atomic submit
findings.suggested_fix: Mark ticket ops as best-effort: catch exceptions, log a structured warning with the change-id and error, post a Gerrit comment on the submitted change as an audit trail, then continue the loop rather than crashing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the configuration mechanism for the await timeout (e.g. 'read from the same `AUTOLANDER_TIMEOUT_SECS` env-var as `land`') so the executor does not need to investigate the existing pattern independently.
findings.finding: The 'configurable' timeout is described without specifying the configuration mechanism (env var, config file, CLI flag, constructor kwarg), leaving the executor to choose an appropriate X — a defaultable gap that should be stated.
findings.location: AC: Bounded await — 'default 30 min, configurable, matching `land`'s timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'configurable via the same mechanism as `land`'s timeout (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_TIMEOUT_SECS` env-var, default 1800 s).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry/backoff policy (e.g., exponential backoff with cap) for Gerrit poll calls and the submit POST, and define what constitutes a retriable vs. terminal error for each.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry/backoff for Gerrit API calls made during the fresh-Verified polling loop or for the submit POST itself. A transient Gerrit 5xx during polling would either silently fail or immediately trigger a hand-back, and a transient failure on POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit could abandon a stack that was ready to land.
findings.location: AC: Bounded await / Ancestor-atomic submit; Files: infra/autolander/loop.py
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: individual Gerrit REST calls retry up to N times with exponential backoff before counting as a poll failure; submit POST retries up to M times; only after exhausting retries does the stack hand back via S2b.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define handling for rebar ticket annotation failure after a successful submit: specify retry policy, and ensure re-running the autolander on an already-submitted change safely re-attempts annotation without attempting a second submit.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the partial-completion scenario where the submit POST succeeds (change lands in Gerrit) but the subsequent rebar ticket annotation fails. The stack is landed but the ticket is un-annotated, and there is no recovery or idempotent re-annotation path specified.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit — 'On success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'
findings.suggested_fix: Store annotation status separately (e.g., a flag in the stack record); on startup/retry, check if change is already merged and re-attempt annotation if the flag is unset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a `depends_on` link from S2c to S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee) to make the build-order dependency explicit, or specify the exact function signature S2c will call so the interface contract is verifiable independently of S2b's internal implementation.
findings.finding: The AC says 'on timeout (CI hung), hand the stack back (S2b mechanism)' but this ticket (S2c) declares no `depends_on` link to S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee). The hand-back symbol/function is defined in S2b's scope within the same file; S2c's implementation cannot call it without the sibling being built first or the interface being pre-agreed.
findings.location: AC — Bounded await bullet: 'hand the stack back (S2b mechanism)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR (or CLAUDE.md section) capturing the fresh-Verified-per-member gate rule and ancestor-atomic tip-submit contract as durable architectural decisions, cross-linking ADR-0041.
findings.finding: The fresh-Verified-per-member gate and ancestor-atomic submit are novel architectural decisions (establishing the R5 gate invariant) but the plan names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture them; only unit tests and a live check are specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Ancestor-atomic submit' and 'Fresh Verified (per member)' bullets
findings.suggested_fix: Create ADR-XXXX 'Fresh-Verified Gate and Ancestor-Atomic Submit' documenting: (1) why per-member SHA verification is required (R5), (2) that `rebase:chain` mints distinct SHAs, (3) the single-tip submit mechanism and its all-or-nothing semantics under FFO, and (4) the timeout/hand-back policy. Reference it from CLAUDE.md under the autolander section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the `rebar` ticket-ops coupling in the plan, define whether ticket annotation failures are fatal or advisory, and consider wrapping the call behind an interface or try/except with explicit fallback.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component runtime dependency (`import rebar` for ticket ops in `loop.py`) without acknowledging the coupling, justifying it, or defining an interface/event boundary to isolate the autolander from rebar internals.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Ancestor-atomic submit' bullet: 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each new failure point (poll timeout, submit error, rebar annotation failure, hand-back event) emits a structured log entry and/or increments a named counter metric, and define at least one alert threshold (e.g., alert if hand-back rate exceeds X% over 1 hour).
findings.finding: No observability (metrics, logs, or alerts) is specified for the new failure points introduced: CI poll timeouts, Gerrit submit failures, rebar annotation failures, or hand-back events. Operators have no visibility into why stacks are being handed back or how often.
findings.location: Files: infra/autolander/loop.py; AC sections: Bounded await, Ancestor-atomic submit

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the CREATE/UPDATE/RETIRE lifecycle for the per-member fresh-Verified tracking state: where it is stored (in-memory vs. durable), who owns it, what happens on process restart, and how it is cleaned up after submit or hand-back.
findings.finding: The in-flight per-member fresh-Verified tracking state (which SHAs have been confirmed Verified during the await window) has no defined durability or lifecycle. If the autolander process restarts mid-await, it is unclear whether the state is lost (causing re-polling from scratch, which is safe but wasteful) or the stack is silently dropped.
findings.location: AC: Fresh Verified (per member) / Bounded await; Files: infra/autolander/loop.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing call-sites that previously triggered submit in `loop.py` (or via the S2 parent path) and confirm each routes through the new fresh-Verified await gate, or explicitly note which are deferred/out of scope.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new fresh-Verified gate on the submit path in loop.py but does not acknowledge whether any existing callers (e.g., orchestrators, retry hooks, or the parent S2 logic) directly invoke the submit path and must be updated or will silently bypass the new gate.
findings.location: Files section / AC – Ancestor-atomic submit
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection 'Caller impact' listing known entry-points (e.g., `land` command handler, retry hook) and assert they all funnel through the new `await_fresh_verified_then_submit` path; if any are out of scope for this ticket, call that out explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how ticket IDs are resolved for the post-submit close/annotate step (e.g., 'parse `rebar-ticket:` trailers from the submitted change's commit message via `GET /changes/{id}?o=CURRENT_COMMIT`') and add a unit test for this path to the Tests AC.
findings.finding: The AC states 'On success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops' but does not specify HOW the ticket IDs are resolved from a submitted Gerrit change — e.g., by parsing `rebar-ticket:` commit trailers from the change's commit message. Without this, the `import rebar` ops step is unimplementable and untestable.
findings.location: AC — Ancestor-atomic submit bullet: 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Ticket IDs are read from `rebar-ticket:` trailers in the submitted commit message (fetched via `GET /changes/{id}?o=CURRENT_COMMIT`); for each ID call `rebar.transition(id, "in_progress", "closed")` and `rebar.comment(id, "Landed as <sha>")`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-handling path in AC3 for submit-time FFO rejection (e.g., Gerrit 409 / FAILED_PRECONDITION): distinguish it from timeout, and specify that the autolander re-triggers rebase (S2b) and re-enters the await loop rather than either dropping the stack or treating it as a generic failure.
findings.finding: The plan's await-then-submit flow has an unhandled TOCTOU window: after the per-member Verified poll passes and before `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` is called, another stack can land on `main` under FFO, making the polled chain non-submittable. Gerrit will return a 409 (conflict/precondition failure) for the submit call. The plan mentions only two outcomes for the submit: success (close/annotate tickets) and timeout (hand back via S2b), but not a submit-time rejection due to FFO displacement — which requires a different recovery path (re-trigger rebase, not just hand back).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit (AC3) and Bounded await (AC2)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the 'Why' section (or inline in AC2) explaining why polling-with-timeout was chosen over an event-driven stream-events listener — e.g., simpler reconnect/state management in a loop.py control-flow model, acceptable latency at this scale, no persistent SSH session required.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for the polling-await approach (repeated GET polls for Verified status) over an event-driven alternative (e.g., listening to Gerrit's stream-events or webhook callbacks for label-change events). The absence of a stated rationale leaves the implementer without guidance on why polling was chosen and what its known trade-offs are.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Bounded await (AC2) / Why section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the bot identity and SSM credential slot used for `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit`, and confirm its Gerrit Submit permission is scoped to the minimum required project/group (not All-Projects admin-for-convenience).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a submit-capable bot action (POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit) — a more-privileged and irreversible Gerrit operation compared to the existing review-bot's vote-casting — but never declares the access posture: which bot identity is used, what SSM credential slot it reads, and whether that identity's Gerrit submit permission is scoped to the minimum (e.g., project-level Submit only, not admin).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Ancestor-atomic submit' bullet; Files: infra/autolander/loop.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the AC or a 'Auth contract' sub-section: 'The autolander authenticates as `<bot-user>` using the HTTP token from SSM `<param-path>`; that account holds Submit permission only on `refs/heads/*` in the `rebar` project (not All-Projects), provisioned by S5.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
